item.title: Private and Public School Enrolment in Ontario
item.description:
I’ve had reason to think a lot about the quality of public education lately. Our five-year old has entered Grade 1 this year and so school has been a huge focus. Plus, with the lockdowns last year, we got to (had to? ) take part in a lot of her schooling, more than in normal years. Mostly we’ve been really happy with the quality of the education, but there are ways in which the experience has been underwhelming. On top of that, there have been a skirmishes in the culture wars in the news lately on the field of public education.

Close to home, the Waterloo Region District School Board has found itself in trouble over a review of the district’s libraries for books deemed ``harmful’’. The Board’s description of the process is here. At the Toronto District School Board, the superintendent felt a student book club should not be discussing the recently published memoirs by Marie Heinein link, nor apparently a book by Nobel Peace Prize winning activist Nadia Murad. At the same time, the government of Ontario is “destreaming” Ontario Grade 9 classes starting next year.

I’ll admit: the combination of stories like this make me, a parent of two kids with a household that values learning pretty intensely a bit nervous about the quality of education my kids are going to get going forward.

The combination of policies above arguably make public education less attractive to families with motivated parents and students because there are potentially fewer opportunities to push the limits of their education. The economist Albert Hirschman described situations like this in a class work Exit, Voice and Loyalty. Consumers of policies, services or goods face a problem when the quality begins to decline. Namely, you have limited tools to express your displeasure at the decline. You can raise your “voice” e.g. express your displeasure by writing letters, signing petitions, etc. Or you can take off to the next firm and take your money elsewhere. For a variety of reasons, “exit” is less effective than “voice”.

So, all of the above got me wondering about whether or not there was any evidence that “exit” was increasing in terms of whether private school enrolment was increasing in Ontario or not.

You would sort of think that data like this would be readily available and it was-ish (to use the parlance of our times). Ontario publishes data on private school enrolmemnt here and data on public school enrolment here but of course what we want is a combined data-set.

I took a couple of hours to force the two data-sets together published for posterity here and the final data are right .

So, to be clear, this is the share of all students (elementary and secondary, Catholic and public) in Ontario who are attending private schools. So, this does account for population growth; this is, effectively, parents using their power of exit over the public school system. It’s definitely peripheral, around 7% of Ontario students are registered in private schools, but it is also clearly increasing.

So, on the one hand, Ontario’s publicly-funded school system is pretty well insulated from exit, but there is a weak signal there. Much more

item.title: Right Wing Populism and Municipal Governance: The Case of Toronto
The Ambiguous Definitions of Open Government
Public Perceptions of Hydraulic Fracturing (Fracking) in Canada
